entries.title: Founders' Square — the ceremony
entries.text: Fifty years of waiting and the vault was empty. Not robbed-looking. Just... empty. The lock turned clean. Dot wants the front page; the town wants an answer. Start with who could open a door without breaking it.

entries.title: Otto Kessler — The Percolator
entries.text: Built like a hillside, talks like a slow pour. The café hears everything in this district eventually. So does Otto.

entries.title: Margie Plum — The Drowsy Lantern
entries.text: Inn's full for the ceremony. New lodger: 'maps and whispers,' asks about treasure at breakfast. Margie quotes people exactly — useful trait in a witness, terrifying in a landlady.

entries.title: Gino Puglisi — fruit stall
entries.text: Forty percent of the stall is grapes. This is not information. This is a threat assessment.

entries.title: Ida Wren — Wren & Wick
entries.text: Made the capsule's lock with her own apprentice hands, fifty years back. Still oils it. Talks to it. The lock, reportedly, has never once let her down. People are another matter, she implies.

entries.title: Evelyn Croft — flower shop
entries.text: Fifty arrangements for fifty years. This year's is ivy — 'not technically a flower, technically a promise.' Wrote that one down word for word.

entries.title: Poppy Finch — event planner
entries.text: Runs on fourteen laminated contingency cards and no sleep. Apologized to a ladder. Her whole career is tonight going right.

entries.title: Warren Holt — Council Guardian
entries.text: Eight years keeping the seat warm for the Vale heir. Rehearses his handover speech to pigeons, who have notes. Seems genuinely glad to be done — worth remembering how rare that is.

entries.title: Julian Vale — the heir
entries.text: Turns 24 this week; takes the chair at the ceremony. Smiles like it's a job. Spins his father's ring when the subject turns to tonight.

entries.title: Clara Vale — the sister
entries.text: Answered my question with a better question, twice. Told me to watch the crowd, not the vault. I intend to steal that technique.

entries.title: Milo Tanaka — junior detective
entries.text: Age 11. Notebook labeled CRIMES? — the question mark doing heavy lifting. Writes exclusively in red ink. A LOT of red ink. Filed under: hm.

entries.title: Constable Bram Tuck
entries.text: Polices by proximity to pastry. Predicted the capsule holds drawings and a tooth. The man has never hurried and has never been late. Respect.

entries.title: Council Hall — the key cabinet
entries.text: The Ceremonial Key was hung back facing the wall, and the dust says it traveled recently. Someone put it back in a hurry, or in the dark, or both.

entries.title: The vault floor — a library of dust
entries.text: Every item left its shape. Matched each void to Poppy's checklist. One detail won't let go: the envelope's void has a SHARP edge. Everything else faded soft over decades. It came out last, and it came out carefully.

entries.title: Poppy — the confession
entries.text: She borrowed the key three nights early, checked everything (MICE, Mr. Gibbons, what if MICE), locked it back, and made a time-stamped laminated inventory. The theft window just shrank to two nights, and Poppy shrank out of the suspect pool.

entries.title: The seal — sketched from memory
entries.text: Green wax. A lantern wreathed in ivy, three drips. Poppy saw it on top of everything, 'important on purpose.' Somebody in this town knows that seal. Find them.

entries.title: Poppy, the morning after
entries.text: Reorganized the contingency binder because something had to be alphabetical today. Filed under: people who cope loudly. Also filed under: people who know something.

entries.title: Margie's quote — exact, as always
entries.text: 'I'm not certain the boy is ready.' One sherry, three nights before the ceremony, quoted to the letter. The town heard doubt. I heard a man checking his own work.

entries.title: The lodger, per Margie
entries.text: A week at the inn, key never on its hook past dark, treasure questions before the eggs. Whispering is his indoor voice.

entries.title: Warren's third draft
entries.text: 'The happiest sentence I will ever say: the waiting is over.' He rewrote it twice because the JOY wasn't right. Men plotting coups edit differently.

entries.title: Forty-seven lighthouses
entries.text: Tickets. Tide tables. A dream deferred eight years and packed anyway. Warren wasn't holding the chair. He was holding the door.

entries.title: The night photograph
entries.text: Night −2, long exposure: lanterns like honey, and a tail at the frame's edge. One weasel tail, in a district with no weasels on the rolls. Warren checked twice. So did I.

entries.title: The loaner camera
entries.text: 'You have your grandfather's eye for people. Borrow mine for places.' Warren's bellows camera now lives in the satchel. The satchel is honored.

entries.title: The Founder's Addition — ten years ago
entries.text: The capsule was legally opened once before: one hour, one dying founder, one green-sealed envelope added. His final wishes, for the 50th. Everyone assumed they named Julian. Assumed.

entries.title: The Ledger Morgue
entries.text: Fifty years of the district in bound volumes and clipping drawers, under my own floor the whole time. The quietest room in town. Archie's desk is down there, one drawer locked.

entries.title: Then & now: the plaque moved
entries.text: The founding photograph against today's square: the dedication plaque migrated a full stride, ten years ago, to make room for the Addition. The vault has opened exactly twice in fifty years — and both times, lawfully. Until now.

entries.title: Ferris Mott — the lodger
entries.text: Believes in the treasure the way other people believe in weather. Winked twice. Tail described, unprompted, as 'heritage couture.' Filed under: watch tonight.

entries.title: The stakeout
entries.text: Hole number six, one upended sack: trowels, jam, maps, no capsule. 'Wrong crimes, folks. Right weasel, wrong crimes.' The town's tidy ending fell apart on a riverbank at midnight.

entries.title: Ferris's testimony — the pivot
entries.text: Night −2: a tall figure, dry under a posh umbrella, used a KEY smooth as butter, moved like the square belonged to him. The red herring turned out to be a lens.

entries.title: The holed sole
entries.text: Followed one distinctive boot from six garden crimes to a flattened watching-spot in the reeds. He wasn't digging at the monument that night. He was WATCHING it.

entries.title: Where the figure stood
entries.text: Warren's balcony plus the tail's place in frame: the figure stood on the river-side line, off the manor shortcut. Light and habit, doing geometry.

entries.title: The fire-escape sightline
entries.text: Stood where the witness stood. The chimney hides the manor side; the lantern strip shows the crossing; and from above, an umbrella hides the whole him. The story holds — and now I know WHY it's incomplete.

entries.title: The torn letter (not for the board's strings)
entries.text: Eight fragments, one unsent apology. It answers nothing and confirms everything. Some evidence is for understanding, not for presenting.

entries.title: Handwriting comparison
entries.text: The looped G, the crossed double-t, the pen pressure. Three features, the way Archie taught. Same hand.

entries.title: Three ghosts on an old map
entries.text: The 1890s survey over the modern district: a filled mill race, a lost market cross — and the original VALE BOAT LANDING, unmarked, straight below the old boathouse. Family water.

questions.text: Who keeps taking Dot's red pens? (One a shift. She counts.)
Who emptied the capsule — and how did they open it without forcing Ida's lock?
WHY TWO KEYS? The founding portrait shows Edmund with a second key at his belt.
Which is true — the doubt Margie heard, or the joy in Warren's speech? (They can't both be the whole truth.)
Ferris watched a key turn at 2 a.m. — but the council key was accounted for. ...Is it the only key?
